text: The initial document was probably sent within the Baltic region (or tested there). It was submitted to VirusTotal from Latvia. The name of the document translated from Russian is "new questioner". It is password-protected with the password: "goodmorning". It uses social engineering to convince the recipient to enable macros through the use of the images, logo and tagline of a newly launched, legitimate VPN tool InvinciBull by cybersecurity company Finjan. The next stage obfuscated JavaScript is extracted from the form caption. The obfuscation of both the macro and the JavaScript is part of this technique, where adversaries disguise their code to evade detection. It involves executing JavaScript from VBScript the execution of VBScript, which in this case is used to launch JavaScript. The deobfuscated JavaScript is actually a backdoor component that directly communicates to the C2 server (in this case hxxps://bing-cdn[.]com) The C2 communication over HTTPS where adversaries communicate over common web protocols to avoid detection. It executes the response The backdoor uses JavaScript to execute commands received from the C2 server, leveraging the eval function to run these commands dynamically.

text:
desktop video capture Adversaries may attempt to take screen captures of the desktop to gather information over the course of an operation.

VNC, Some of its capabilities include VNC.

Logs key strokes for Key configured processes and logger sends them to the command and control (C2) server Adversaries may log user keystrokes to intercept credentials as the user types them.

CARBANAK communicates to its C2 servers via pseudo-HTTP or a custom binary protocol. Adversaries may use an OSI non-application layer protocol for communication between host and C2 server or among infected hosts within a network.

Messages are encrypted using Microsoft's implementation of RC2 in CBC mode with PKCS#5 padding. The encrypted message is then Base64 encoded Most of CARBANAK's strings are encrypted in order to make analysis more difficult.

A 256-bit AES session key is generated and used to encrypt both message headers and bodies separately. Initially, the key is sent to the C2 server with the entire message and headers encrypted with the RSA key exchange algorithm. All subsequent messages are encrypted with AES in CBC mode. Adversaries may employ an encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic rather than relying on any inherent protections provided by a communication protocol.

This command downloads an executable from the C2 and directly runs it in memory. Adversaries may inject code into processes in order to evade process-based defenses as well as possibly elevate privileges.

FIN7 uses CARBANAK as a post-exploitation tool in later phases of an intrusion to cement their foothold in a network and maintain access, frequently using the video command to monitor users and learn about the victim network, as well as the tunnel command to proxy connections into isolated portions of the victim environment FIN7 uses CARBANAK, specifically its video and tunnel commands, to maintain access and monitor the network.

FIN7 has consistently utilized legally purchased code signing certificates to sign their CARBANAK payloads. Adversaries may bypass process and/or signature-based defenses by proxying execution of malicious content with signed, or otherwise trusted, binaries.

FIN7 Spear Phishing Campaign Targets Personnel Involved in SEC Filings FIN7 uses spear phishing emails

text:
FIN7 typically initiated its cyberattacks by delivering a "phishing" email to a company employee. Each email included an attached file, often an innocuous-appearing Microsoft Word document, with embedded malware.

Once infected, the compromised victim computer connected to one of FIN7's command and control servers located throughout the world.

Through a specially designed control panel, FIN7 could download a wide array of additional malware to the victim computer.

FIN7 incorporated and adapted the notorious Carbanak malware for persistent access and control.

FIN7's malware allowed its members to conduct surveillance on company employees, including taking screen shots.

The malware also recorded videos of desktop activity, enabling more detailed surveillance and data theft.

enabling them to secretly steal credentials and other network information.

FIN7 then used its unauthorized access to the victim's computer system to locate and extract various information and property of value, such as financial information and caches of customer payment card data.

The text within the email simulated a legitimate businessrelated message in order to lead the recipient employee to open the attachment and unwittingly activate the malware that would infect the computer.

BOOSTWRITE – an in-memory-only dropper BOOSTWRITE retrieves an encryption key from a remote server that decrypts embedded payloads using an encryption key retrieved from a remote server at runtime The use of encryption to protect the embedded payloads is a form of obfuscation, making it harder for analysts to understand or detect the malicious content without the correct decryption key.

The malware loads into the same process as the Command Center process by abusing the DLL load order of the legitimate Aloha utility The malware is effectively injecting itself into the process. This allows the malware to execute within the context of a trusted application, making it harder to detect and more likely to bypass security controls.

BOOSTWRITE is a loader crafted to be launched via abuse of the DLL search order of applications which load the legitimate 'Dwrite.dll' provided by the Microsoft DirectX Typography Services. The application loads the 'gdi' library, which loads the 'gdiplus' library, which ultimately loads 'Dwrite'. BOOSTWRITE is crafted to exploit the DLL search order used by applications, specifically targeting those that load the Dwrite.dll (a legitimate Microsoft DirectX Typography Services DLL). The application follows a typical load order (gdi -> gdiplus -> Dwrite), and the attacker places a malicious Dwrite.dll in a directory that the application will search first, causing it to load the malicious DLL instead of the legitimate one.

Once loaded, DWrite.dll connects to a hard-coded IP and BOOSTWRITE communicates over the internet using a common protocol to connect to a hardcoded IP address and retrieve the decryption key and IV. This technique involves using standard web protocols to communicate with a command-and-control (C2) server.

it retrieves a decryption key and initialization vector (IV) to decrypt two embedded payloads DLLs The process of embedding and encrypting DLL payloads within the DWrite.dll file and then decrypting them using a custom key and IV retrieved from a remote server is similar to software packing. This technique is used to protect the payloads from detection until they are unpacked and executed.

the malware first generates a random file name to be used as a text log under the current user's %TEMP% directory; this filename starts with ~rdf and is followed by a set of random numbers This technique covers instances where adversaries create or generate files at runtime, often with randomized or unpredictable names to evade detection or hinder analysis. The use of random file names can make it more difficult for defenders to identify and track malicious files.

the malware scans its own image to find the location of a 32-byte long multi-XOR key which is used to decode data inside its body BOOSTWRITE scans its own image to find and use a multi-XOR key to decode data within its body. The decoded data includes the IP address and port needed to retrieve the encryption key and IV. This technique involves deobfuscating or decoding files or data to extract useful information, such as decryption keys.

The encryption algorithm uses the ChaCha stream cipher with a 256-bit key and 64-bit IV BOOSTWRITE uses the ChaCha stream cipher, a symmetric encryption algorithm, to encrypt and decrypt the payloads. It retrieves a decryption key and an initialization vector (IV) from a remote server after connecting to a hardcoded IP address and port. This falls under the category of using symmetric cryptography for securing communications.

Once the key and the IV are downloaded the malware decrypts the embedded payloads The malware decrypts embedded payloads using a key and IV, which falls under this technique. Decrypting and then loading a payload into memory without writing it to disk is a tactic to avoid detection by file-based security tools.

The payloads are expected to be PE32.DLLs which, if the tests pass, are loaded into memory without touching the filesystem The downloaded and decrypted payloads are PE32.DLL files that are likely malicious. The malware performs sanity checks to ensure these files are intact and correct before proceeding to load them.

RDFSNIFFER is a module loaded by BOOSTWRITE which allows an attacker to monitor and tamper with legitimate connections made via NCR Corporation's 'Aloha Command Center Client' (RDFClient) RDFSNIFFER's interactions with RDFClient and potentially monitoring network traffic or connections align with using application layer protocols for communication.

RDFSNIFFER loads into the same process as the legitimate RDFClient by abusing the utility's DLL load order, launching each time the 'Aloha Command Center Client' is executed on an impacted system This describes how RDFSNIFFER injects itself into the process of RDFClient, which aligns with the process injection technique

When the RDFSNIFFER module is loaded by BOOSTWRITE it hooks several Win32 API functions intended to enable it to tamper with NCR Aloha Command Center Client sessions or hijack elements of its user-interface Hooking Win32 API functions can be part of process injection techniques, where the malware modifies the behavior of the legitimate application to perform its own functions.

this enables the malware to alter the user's last input time to ensure application sessions do not time out. By tampering with the NCR Aloha Command Center Client's user-interface, the malware could be disguising its malicious actions as normal application behavior.

This module also contains a backdoor component that enables it to inject commands into an active RDFClient session The backdoor component interacts with the RDFClient session, potentially using web protocols or other application layer protocols to send and receive commands.

This backdoor allows an attacker to upload, download, execute and/or delete arbitrary files The ability to upload and download files is directly related to this technique, which involves transferring tools or files into or out of a compromised environment.

CARBANAK

BABYMETAL,

Cybercriminals installed rogue CCTV cameras and used them to record the PINs people used to unlock their phones

It relies on the Gri"on JScript backdoor Attackers use these interpreters to execute malicious commands on compromised systems.

Cobalt/Meterpreter This technique is used in conjunction with Cobalt Strike (Cobalt/Meterpreter) to exploit vulnerabilities and execute payloads on targeted systems, especially in post-exploitation scenarios.

PowerShell Empire Attackers use these interpreters to execute malicious commands on compromised systems.

Carbanak

spear-phishing campaigns These groups use effective spear-phishing campaigns with malicious Microsoft Office documents to deliver their payloads.

well-known Microsoft O"ice exploits generated by their exploitation framework The use of well-known Microsoft Office exploits generated by exploitation frameworks is a common tactic. These exploits do not require zero-day vulnerabilities but instead rely on the fact that many systems remain unpatched against older, well-documented flaws.

they use documents with exploits Documents with exploits are used to execute malicious code on the victim's system. These documents typically exploit vulnerabilities in software like Microsoft Office to gain initial access.

that !rst load a small downloader and then a Cobalt beacon. The initial exploit is designed to load a small downloader, which then downloads additional malicious components (such as the Cobalt beacon) from an external server.

JS sni"ing (MageCarting) in order to obtain data about payment cards directly from websites JS snifing (MageCarting) involves injecting malicious JavaScript into a website, often through compromised third-party services or direct website exploits. This allows attackers to intercept and steal payment card data directly from users interacting with the website.

FIN7 sent spear phishing emails to personnel involved with United States Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) filings at multiple organizations. FIN7's use of spear-phishing emails with malicious attachments to target individuals with access to valuable SEC filings.

FIN7's spear phishing emails that leveraged hidden shortcut files (LNK files). FIN7 used spear-phishing emails containing LNK files to trick victims into initiating the infection process.

leveraged hidden shortcut files (LNK files) to initiate the infection. The use of LNK files as a method of delivering malware that requires the victim to execute the file.

VBScript functionality launched by mshta.exe to infect the victim. The mshta.exe process and VBScript execution are used to exploit the system for code execution without relying on Office macros.

FIN7's use of the CARBANAK backdoor as a postexploitation tool to cement their foothold in a network and maintain access to victim environments. The CARBANAK backdoor allows FIN7 to maintain remote access to victim systems, giving them control over compromised environments for extended periods.

text:
The emails were efficient social engineering attempts that appealed to a vast number of human emotions (fear, stress, anger, etc.) to elicit a response from their victims This reflects the use of spear-phishing campaigns.

We have seen two types of documents sent to victims in these spear phishing campaigns. This reflects the use of spear-phishing campaigns to deliver malicious documents.

exploits the INCLUDEPICTURE feature of Microsoft Word The use of the INCLUDEPICTURE feature to gather information from a document represents a method of executing malicious content when the user interacts with the document.

to get context information about the victim's computer, and the availability and version number of Microsoft Word. Gathering information about the victim's computer, such as the availability and version number of Microsoft Word, is an example of system information discovery.

The second one, which in many cases is an Office document protected with a trivial password, such as "12345", "1234", etc., uses macros to execute a GRIFFON implant on the target's computer The use of macros to execute malicious code (the GRIFFON implant).

In various cases, the associated macro also scheduled tasks to make GRIFFON persistent. Scheduling tasks via macros to ensure persistence of the GRIFFON implant is a common technique used to maintain access to a compromised system.

The GRIFFON implant is a lightweight JScript validator style implant without any persistence mechanism The GRIFFON implant is written in JScript, meaning it uses JavaScript to execute commands and scripts.

The first module downloaded by the GRIFFON malware to the victim's computer is an information-gathering JScript the module is written in JScript, a scripting language used for command execution.

This module mainly relies on WMI and Windows objects to deliver results The use of WMI (Windows Management Instrumentation) indicates that the malware queries system information through this interface for reconnaissance purposes.

more than 20 artifacts are retrieved from the system by this implant during the reconnaissance stage, Gathering details about the system, such as operating system version and environment, is a form of system discovery.

from the date and time of operating system installation and membership in a Windows domain Retrieving domain membership information is part of network configuration discovery.

a list of and the resolutions of the workstation's monitors Collecting information about hardware configurations

The second module is used by the operators to execute an obfuscated PowerShell script PowerShell script is used to run the obfuscated script.

contains a Meterpreter downloader widely known as "Tinymet". This downloader, seen in past FIN7 campaigns, downloads a one-byte XOR-encrypted (eg. with the key equal to 0x50 or 0x51) piece of meterpreter shellcode to execute An obfuscated PowerShell script and XOR-encrypted Meterpreter shellcode are downloaded into the victim's environment.

XOR-encrypted (eg. with the key equal to 0x50 or 0x51) The use of XOR encryption and obfuscation of the shellcode directly relates to this technique.

allows the operators to take a screenshot of the remote system capturing screenshots of the victim's system

it also drops a PowerShell script on the workstation to execute. The script executes an open-source .NET class used for taking a screenshot. a PowerShell script on the workstation to execute, which uses an open-source .NET class to take a screenshot.

If the victim appears valuable to the attackers, a GRIFFON implant installer is pushed to the victim's workstation Pushing a GRIFFON implant installer to the victim's workstation, which involves transferring files into the target system.

This module stores another instance of the GRIFFON implant inside the registry to achieve persistence storing an instance of the GRIFFON implant in the registry

AveMaria is a classic infostealer bot that collects all possible credentials The bot collects credentials from various software, which can include valid accounts for different services.

can act as a keylogger. AveMaria acts as a keylogger, which involves capturing keystrokes made by the user to collect sensitive information.

the cyber criminals use spearphishing emails with various types of attachments The use of spear-phishing emails to deliver malware falls under phishing techniques.

MS Office documents or spreadsheet files exploiting some known vulnerability like CVE-2017-11882, Exploiting known vulnerabilities in documents, such as CVE-2017-11882, to execute malware.

FIN7 phishing lures implements hidden shortcut files (LNK files) to initiate the infection The use of LNK files and VBScript, along with mshta.exe, are methods used to exploit the client system for execution of the malware.

FIN7 is targeting organizations with spear phishing emails containing either a malicious DOCX or RTF file – two versions of the same LNK file and VBScript technique targeting individuals or organizations with phishing emails that contain malicious attachments, such as DOCX or RTF files. The goal is to deceive the recipient into opening the attachment and executing the embedded malware.

By requiring this unique interaction – doubleclicking on the image and clicking the "Open" button in the security warning popup – the phishing lure attempts to evade dynamic detection as many sandboxes are not configured to simulate that specific user action. An adversary may rely upon specific actions by a user in order to gain execution.

the script creates a named schedule task for persistence to launch "58d2a83f7778d5.36783181.vbs" every 25 minutes.

The combined script from Word textbox drops the following components: \Users\ [user_name]\Intel\58d2a83f7778d5.36783181.vbs \Users\ [user_name]\Intel\58d2a83f777942.26535794.ps1 \Users\ [user_name]\Intel\58d2a83f777908.23270411.vbs

This VBScript checks if the "58d2a83f777942.26535794.ps1" PowerShell script is running using WMI queries and, if not, launches it. This technique involves identifying running processes on a system. If the process is not found, the script then starts it, ensuring the required script is active.

"58d2a83f777942.26535794.ps1" is a multilayer obfuscated PowerShell script, which launches shellcode for a Cobalt Strike stager This TTP involves the use of PowerShell scripts for execution. The script mentioned is heavily obfuscated and is used to execute shellcode.

The shellcode retrieves an additional payload by connecting to the following C2 server using DNS: aaa.stage.14919005.www1.proslr3[.]com the use of DNS for command and control (C2) communications. The shellcode retrieves further payloads by connecting to a C2 server using DNS queries.

Once a successful reply is received from the command and control (C2) server, the PowerShell script executes the embedded Cobalt Strike shellcode.

"58d2a83f777942.26535794.ps1" is a multilayer obfuscated PowerShell script, which launches shellcode for a Cobalt Strike stager Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

info: Sends victim machine information (OS, Processor, BIOS and running processes) using WMI queries gathering information about the victim's system, such as operating system details, processor information, BIOS version, and running processes. This is done using WMI queries, which helps the attacker understand the environment and plan further actions.

processList: Send list of process running identifying and listing the processes currently running on the victim's system

screenshot: Takes screen shot of victim machine capturing screenshots of the victim's desktop

runvbs: Executes a VB script The use of VBScript

runps1: Executes PowerShell script the use of PowerShell scripts for execution

delete: Delete the specified file the delete function is used to remove specified files

the document creates two scheduled tasks and creates one auto-start registry entry pointing to the LNK file creating tasks or jobs that run at specified intervals or times to maintain persistence on the victim system

the document creates two scheduled tasks and creates one auto-start registry entry pointing to the LNK file Adversaries may achieve persistence by adding a program to a startup folder or referencing it with a Registry run key.

encoded using the following technique, represented in pseudo code: Function send_data(data) random_string = custom_function_to_generate_random_string Adversaries may employ an encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic rather than relying on any inherent protections provided by a communication protocol.

Astra. The panel, written in PHP, functions as a script-management system Using PHP for management and control of attack scripts fits under the use of scripting languages for execution and control in the attack lifecycle.

pushing attack scripts down to compromised computers The panel pushing attack scripts down to compromised systems indicates transferring malicious tools or files to target systems.

The attackers gain an initial foothold on targeted machines via phishing emails containing malicious attachments Attackers send targeted emails containing malicious attachments to entice victims into opening them and executing the payload.

The emails are often industryspecific and crafted to entice a victim to open the message and execute the attached document Victims are tricked into opening and executing the attached document, enabling the attackers to gain an initial foothold.

malware that drops files and executes SQL scripts on the host system SQLRat uses SQL scripts to execute commands on the host system, which is an uncommon method but falls under the use of SQL as a command interpreter.

Once they are deleted by the attackers' code, there is nothing left to be forensically recovered. After SQL scripts are executed, the attackers' code deletes the files, leaving minimal forensic artifacts, making recovery difficult.

DNSbot, which is used to exchange commands and push data to and from compromised machines DNSbot uses DNS to send and receive commands and data between the compromised machine and the attacker's command-and-control (C2) server.

can also switch to encrypted channels such as HTTPS or SSL DNSbot has the ability to switch to encrypted communication channels such as HTTPS or SSL when necessary.

The campaigns maintain persistence on machines by creating two daily scheduled task entries Creating scheduled task entries to maintain persistence on compromised machines.

Once a user has double-clicked the embedded image, the form executes a VB setup script The lure document tricks the user into executing a malicious file.

The script writes files to the path %appdata%\Roaming\Microsoft\Templates\, then creates two task entries Creating scheduled task entries to maintain persistence.

The scripts are responsible for deobfuscating and executing the main JavaScript file mspromo.dot. Execution of malicious JavaScript code after deobfuscation.

The file uses a character insertion obfuscation technique, making it appear to contain Chinese characters The use of obfuscation techniques to hide the true nature of malicious code.

execute scripts This covers the execution of scripts, particularly those that drop and execute files on a system.

The SQLRat script is designed to make a direct SQL connection to a Microsoft database controlled by the attackers SQLRat uses a direct SQL connection to retrieve and execute files from a remote database, indicating the use of a web-based protocol to communicate with a malicious database.

The script retrieves an item from the bindata table and writes the file to disk SQLRat retrieves and writes files to disk, which can then be executed.

FIN7 leverages a number of targeted phishing techniques to initially exploit victims in the retail sector.
The actor primarily utilize malicious shortcut files (LNK) or visual basic scripts (VBS or VBE) to achieve code execution from within their lure The actor relies on users to open malicious LNK or Visual Basic script (VBS/VBE) files embedded within documents, triggering the infection.
The use of Visual Basic scripts (VBS or VBE) to execute malicious code within the victim system.
FIN7 appears to have pivoted from using OLE embedded LNK files to using OLE embedded CMD files. When executed, the CMD file writes JScript to "tt.txt" FIN7 uses CMD files embedded within documents, which require user interaction to execute malicious code.
The batch script then copies itself to "pp.txt", also under the current user's home directory, before running WScript using the JScript engine on the file The CMD file writes and then executes JScript code using the WScript engine to evaluate further instructions from the file.
The attacker now obfuscates that name and continues to break up the base64 string into multiple strings within an array The attacker continues to obfuscate the codebase by breaking up the base64-encoded string into multiple parts and renaming variables to evade detection.

text:
the initial infection vector is a malicious Word document attached to a phishing email that is well-tailored to the targeted business use of a spear phishing email with an attached malicious Word document to initiate an attack. The document is crafted to target specific business interests or individuals

The Word document executes a fileless attack that uses DNS queries to deliver the next shellcode stage (Meterpreter) use of DNS queries as a communication method to deliver and execute payloads in a fileless manner.

The attached .rtf file uses OLE This TTP refers to the exploitation of vulnerabilities in client applications to execute malicious code. In this case, the .rtf file uses Object Linking and Embedding (OLE) to execute code.

executes obfuscated JavaScript code using JavaScript to execute malicious code. The obfuscated JavaScript in the document is executed to deliver the payload.

The first stage JavaScript copies additional JavaScript code snippets in txt format from the RTF document into a random directory "C:\Users\<User Name>\<Random guid>\". The same code snippets are combined into a second stage JavaScript in "C:\Users\<User Name>\". avaScript is used to handle and execute code snippets.

the first stage JavaScript creates a scheduled task that executes the second stage code within a minute The scheduled task is used here to delay the execution of the second stage code, helping to evade detection.

an additional scheduled task "AdobeFlashSync" is created for persistency The task named "AdobeFlashSync" is used to repeatedly execute actions, including recreating JavaScript code and executing a PowerShell script, thereby maintaining persistence on the victim's system.

recreating the JavaScript code JavaScript is used as part of the attack chain, specifically to generate and execute additional scripts. This aligns with the technique of using JavaScript to perform malicious actions.

which later will create and execute a PowerShell script (described below). This involves using PowerShell scripts as part of the infection chain. The PowerShell script is created and executed as a subsequent stage of the attack.

Afterwards, it deletes its own JavaScript code traces removing files or traces of the attack from the host to cover the tracks and avoid detection

The PowerShell script executes a compressed first stage PowerShell child process, which then performs a second stage PowerShell process the PowerShell script is used to launch a series of PowerShell processes, demonstrating how PowerShell can be leveraged for multiple stages of execution.

The latter PowerShell injects a shellcode into its own process using well-known CreateThread and VirtualAlloc techniques the PowerShell script is using techniques like CreateThread and VirtualAlloc for shellcode injection

FIN7 implemented a shellcode that gets the next stage shellcode using the DNS messaging technique directly from memory The shellcode retrieves the next stage of the attack by leveraging DNS queries to fetch additional malicious code, demonstrating the use of DNS for covert data exfiltration or payload delivery.

the shellcode deletes the 'MZ' prefix from within a very important part of the shellcode. This prefix indicates it may be a dll, and its deletion helps the attack to evade memory scanning solutions. the shellcode is encrypted

final_triplets.subject: powershell script
final_triplets.object: compressed first stage powershell child process

reviewed_triplets.object: compressed first stage powershell child process

text:
The shim injected a malicious in-memory patch into the Services Control Manager ("services.exe") process, and then spawned a CARBANAK backdoor process. FIN7 leveraged an application shim database to achieve persistent access on systems in multiple environments.

FIN7 used a custom Base64 encoded PowerShell script, which ran the "sdbinst.exe" utility to register a custom shim database file containing a patch onto a system. obtained an additional shellcode payload stored in a registry key.

The second stage shellcode launched the CARBANAK DLL (stored in a registry key), which spawned an instance of Service Host ("svchost.exe") and injected itself into that process. The new "ScRegisterTCPEndpoint" function (shellcode) contained a reference to the path of "\REGISTRY\MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\DRM", which is a registry location where additional malicious shellcode and the CARBANAK DLL payload was stored on the system.

The shellcode stored within the registry path "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\DRM" used the API function "RtlDecompressBuffer" to decompress the payload. Once loaded in memory, it created a new process named "svchost.exe" that contained the CARBANAK DLL.

FIN7 used Cobalt Strike's DNS C2 as their method of maintaining access inside target environments DNS tunneling is used to establish and maintain a covert channel for C2 communication, evading traditional detection mechanisms that might monitor for suspicious IP traffic.

C2 method leveraged a different encrypted communication channel.

the threat actors would spawn a secondary C2 component during their off-hour periods in an apparent attempt to maintain access should the DNS C2 be detected.

DNS C2 leverages malicious DNS TXT and A RRs (resource records) queries which traverse standard recursive DNS channels and terminate at an attacker's authoritative DNS server. Queries containing data or commands are sent as DNS TXT (Text) or A (Address) resource records, making the traffic blend into normal DNS activity. This is effective in environments with restrictive firewalls, web filters, or proxies, such as POS networks in the retail or hospitality sectors, where DNS traffic is often allowed and less scrutinized.

the actors used PowerShell scripts to deploy DNS TXT record stagers into memory.

During execution, PowerShell would make iterative DNS TXT queries, which would return encrypted data to be concatenated and then executed in memory.

After staging, the attacker would shift to the use of DNS A resource records. When idle, the malware would make requests to the attacker-controlled domain with a pattern matching [SessionID].MaliciousDomain.com.

FIN7 would frequently use the psexec_psh command within Cobalt Strike, which uses the RPC Service Controller protocol to create a service on a remote host with the binary path set to execute a malicious PowerShell command.

text:
This starts as a spear phishing email sent to the initial target FIN7 starts their attack with spear phishing emails to deliver malware. The emails are crafted to provoke a reaction from the target, often using emotional triggers such as

The exchange then leads to an initial document delivery that is usually benign sending attachments as part of a spear phishing campaign. The initial document may appear benign but includes tracking mechanisms.

possesses a tracking pixel that allows the actor to verify whether or not the target is opening attachments, and if so can provide initial visibility into the system of the target the document or tracking mechanism provides information about network connections or system details

The second document the attacker sends is usually the one with the actual payload. This document has either a VBA macro or an embedded OLE which persuades the target to run these in order to drop the first stage loaders Exploiting vulnerabilities in client applications to execute malicious payloads.

An adversary may rely upon a user opening a malicious file in order to gain execution.

Adversaries may abuse Visual Basic (VB) for execution.

Spear phishing a target with a document loaded with a malware payload which as of May 2018 has been the Gri"on payload, or a combination of SQLRat and DNSBot historically. sending phishing emails with malicious attachments to target individuals.

To establish persistence, the group then injects a scheduled task to reestablish the C2 connection to Cobalt Strike which historically is run twice a day Creating scheduled tasks to ensure persistence and reestablish connections is covered under this TTP.

From there the adversary loads process injection tools or memory scrapers (commonly mimikatz) and pivots using tools like powersploit, RDP services, and PowerAdmin Exec. Adversaries may inject code into processes in order to evade process-based defenses as well as possibly elevate privileges.

Adversaries may attempt to access credential material stored in the process memory of LSASS.

Adversaries may use Valid Accounts to log into a computer using the Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP). The adversary may then perform actions as the logged-on user.

reviewed_triplets.subject: spear phishing emails

text:
FIN7 would gain access to the POS environment primarily using PSExec. FIN7 used PSExec, a legitimate remote administration tool from Sysinternals, to execute processes on the POS systems remotely, which is part of their lateral movement strategy to spread malware across a network.

combined with a custom PowerShell script to deploy their payload. A custom PowerShell script is used to execute and manage payload delivery, showcasing the use of PowerShell as an execution platform. PowerShell scripts are commonly abused by attackers to execute arbitrary code and evade detection due to their presence in most Windows environments.

The weaponized PowerShell script was built to load an embedded DLL using reflective injection. Reflective DLL injection is a technique where a DLL is injected into a process without touching disk, which helps evade traditional security controls.

the script executed code from PowerSploit designed to resolve Windows API functions necessary for performing the injection. The use of Windows API functions to perform the injection, manage memory, and create threads is a critical aspect of reflective DLL injection. By directly accessing low-level system calls, FIN7 ensures the payload remains hidden in memory.

the script would allocate memory in its current process and use the System.Runtime.Interopservices.Marshal class to copy code into the newly allocated memory. Creating a thread to execute the bootstrap code for loading a DLL is an example of process injection. This technique is used to inject malicious code into another process's address space, which helps evade detection by running malicious code within a legitimate process.

Once injected, the payload proceeded to write a binary encoded blob into the registry key HKLM\Software\Microsoft\DRM. This technique involves modifying the Windows Registry to store malicious data or configurations. FIN7 uses this method to write a binary blob into the registry, which is later accessed and executed as part of their payload, making it harder to detect or remove since the data isn't stored on disk.

install persistence using a custom Shim targeting services.exe and adding it to the shim database by executing sdbinst.exe. Application Shimming is a technique that involves modifying how applications are executed on the system by creating a custom compatibility shim. In this case, FIN7 creates a shim for services.exe to ensure the malicious payload is executed whenever the service starts, achieving persistence. This is done by adding the shim to the shim database using the sdbinst.exe tool.

the POS scraping capability will attempt to inject itself into svchost.exe, wuauclt.exe or explorer.exe (in that order). Injecting malicious code into the memory of legitimate processes such as svchost.exe, wuauclt.exe, and explorer.exe.

Upon successful injection, the scraper enumerates running processes, comparing them to a list provided via a text file on disk. This describes enumerating running processes on the infected system, which is a process discovery technique. The malware checks for specific processes to determine which ones it can target for scraping sensitive data.

Once the scraping capability has identified a process of interest, the payload will read memory from the process and extract track one and track two data formats. Memory scraping is a method used to extract sensitive data, such as payment card information, directly from the memory of running processes. The malware specifically targets track one and track two data, which are commonly found in credit card transactions and contain information like account numbers, expiration dates, and service codes.

Once extracted, the malware encrypts the data and writes it to disk for later collection by the threat actors. After scraping the sensitive data, the malware encrypts the stolen information before writing it to disk. This ensures that even if the files are discovered by security teams, the data remains unreadable without the decryption key. Obfuscation or encryption is a common tactic to hide stolen data from detection.

final_triplets.subject: payload
final_triplets.object: track one and track two data

text:
JScript backdoor called Bateleur The Bateleur backdoor, written in JScript, is executed as part of the attack chain, allowing remote code execution.

The new macros and Bateleur backdoor use sophisticated anti-analysis and sandbox evasion techniques FIN7's Bateleur backdoor and the updated macros use sophisticated methods to detect and evade sandboxes or analysis environments.

the Vrst FIN7 change we observed was in the obfuscation technique found in their usual document attachments FIN7 changed their obfuscation techniques in the document attachments to hide malicious content.

drop a previously undocumented JScript backdoor, which we have named "Bateleur" FIN7 transitioned from their previous GGLDR script to a new JScript backdoor called Bateleur.

The email is sent from an Outlook.com account, and the attachment document lure also matches that information by claiming "This document is encrypted by Outlook Protect Service". The email contains a malicious attachment designed to deceive the recipient into opening the file. The use of a service-specific lure, such as claiming encryption by Outlook Protect Service or Google Documents Protect Service, is a form of social engineering to persuade the victim to interact with the file.

The email contains a macro-laden Word document. The macro-laden Word document requires the user to open the file and enable the macro for execution, leading to the extraction of the JScript payload.

The caption contains a "|*|"-delimited obfuscated JScript payload The malicious payload is a JScript that is extracted and executed. JScript is a common scripting language used for automation and code execution.

The macro Vrst extracts the JScript from the caption then saves the content to debug.txt in the current user's temporary folder (%TMP%) The macro writes the extracted JScript payload to the debug.txt file in the temporary folder, likely with the intent to delete this file after execution to evade detection and forensic analysis.

the macro creates a scheduled task The macro creates a scheduled task to ensure the execution of the JScript payload.

execute debug.txt as a JScript. The malicious payload is a JScript that is extracted and executed. JScript is a common scripting language used for automation and code execution.

Finally, the macro sleeps for 10 seconds then deletes the malicious scheduled task his involves removing files that may indicate malicious activity, such as deleting scheduled tasks or other artifacts after they have been executed to avoid detection.

The malicious JScript has robust capabilities that include anti-sandbox functionality, anti-analysis (obfuscation) The adversary uses obfuscation to hide malicious code or make it harder to analyze.

retrieval of infected system information The malware gathers information about the infected system.

listing of running processes The malware lists running processes on the system.

execution of  custom commands and PowerShell scripts The malware uses PowerShell scripts for executing commands.

loading of EXEs and DLLs The malware loads executables (EXEs) and dynamic-link libraries (DLLs) into memory.

taking screenshots The malware takes screenshots of the infected system.

uninstalling and updating itself The malware can update itself by downloading new versions from the command and control server.

possibly the ability to exVltrate passwords, although the latter requires an additional module from the command and control server (C&C) The malware exfiltrates credentials (passwords) from the victim's machine, though it requires an additional module from the C&C server.

it creates a scheduled task "GoogleUpdateTaskMachineSystem" for persistence using the following command pattern: schtasks /Create /f /tn "GoogleUpdateTaskMachineSystem" /tr "wscript.exe //b /e:jscript C:\Users\[user account]\AppData\Local\Temp\{[hex]-[hex]-[hex]-[hex]-[hex]}\debug.txt" /sc minute /mo 5 This technique involves the use of scheduled tasks to execute programs or scripts at a specific time or interval for persistence.

Bateleur has anti-sandbox features but they do not appear to be used at this time.  This includes detection of Virtualbox, VMware, or Parallels via SMBIOSBIOSVersion and any of the following strings in DeviceID This technique involves malware checking the environment for signs of being run in a virtualized or sandboxed environment to avoid detection or analysis. Common checks include identifying virtual machines or sandbox-specific indicators.

Return running process list (name + id) Listing the processes that are currently running on a system.

Return various information about the infected machine, such as computer and domain name, OS, screen size, This technique involves gathering information about the system to better understand the environment or to gather information useful for further attacks. This can include details like the computer name, operating system, and more.

net view This technique involves scanning network services to gather information about the network and potentially identify other systems and

Delete installation Vle and path and remove scheduled task Removing installation file and path and remove scheduled tasks.

Perform a "load_exe" request to the C&C to retrieve an EXE This technique involves transferring tools or other files to a compromised system. In this case, the load_exe request to the command and control (C&C) server to retrieve an executable file.

write a cmd.exe command to a Vle named debug.cmd and then execute debug.cmd with cmd.exe This technique involves writing and executing commands or scripts. In this case, writing a cmd.exe command to a file and then executing it.

Perform a "load_exe" request to C&C to retrieve an EXE, save it as debug.log the load_exe request to the command and control (C&C) server to retrieve an executable file.

execute the EXE via WMI This technique involves using Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to execute commands or scripts. Here, WMI is used to execute the retrieved EXE.

Perform a "load_dll" request to the C&C to retrieve a DLL, save it as debug.backup the load_dll request to the command and control (C&C) server retrieves a DLL.

write a regsvr32 command to a Vle named debug.cmd and then execute debug.cmd with cmd.exe a regsvr32 command is written to a file and then executed.

Perform a "load_cmd" request to the C&C to retrieve a command to execute A command file is retrieved from the command and control (C&C) server.

create temp Vle named log_[date].cmd containing command to execute, execute the command and sleep for 55 seconds a command is executed via a batch file

Send Vle output to the C&C via a POST request Using web protocols (HTTP/HTTPS) for command and control communication. Sending the output of the executed command to the C&C server.

remove the temporary command Vle removing the temporary command file

Perform a "load_powershell" request to the C&C to retrieve a command to execute A command file is retrieved from the command and control (C&C) server.

create a temp Vle named log_[date].log containing a PowerShell command to execute, execute the command, and sleep for 55 seconds Executing commands via PowerShell.

Send Vle output to the C&C via a POST request Sending the output of the executed command to the C&C server.

executes a PowerShell command via WMI Using WMI to execute commands

Take a screenshot and save it as screenshot.png in the install_path Capturing screenshots of the victim's machine.

Perform a "load_pass" request to the C&C to retrieve a PowerShell command A command file is retrieved from the command and control (C&C) server.

PowerShell command containing a payload A PowerShell command containing a payload

capable of retrieving user account credentials Retrieving credentials

The Bateleur C&C protocol occurs over HTTPS and is fairly straightforward with no additional encoding or obfuscation. Bateleur uses HTTP POST requests with a URI of "/?page=wait" while the backdoor is waiting for instructions. Bateleur communicates with its C&C server using web protocols (HTTPS and HTTP POST), which is common for exfiltrating data and receiving commands.

This document is used in phishing campaigns to execute a series of scripting languages Attackers use email attachments containing malicious files to execute a series of scripting languages containing multiple obfuscation mechanisms and advanced techniques to bypass traditional security mechanisms. The use of script languages to execute malicious code.
FIN7 uses techniques such as file obfuscation to avoid detection by traditional security tools.
The document contains messages enticing the user to click on an embedded object that executes scripts Attackers rely on user actions to open malicious files or click on embedded objects, triggering the malware execution.
This malware is then used to steal passwords from popular browsers and mail clients Attackers steal saved login credentials from web browsers, gaining access to accounts.
FIN7's malware uses legitimate web protocols such as HTTP/HTTPS to communicate with its command-and-control servers.
a new infection vector technique using an RTF document containing an embedded JavaScript OLE object Attackers deliver phishing emails containing malicious attachments, in this case, an RTF document embedded with a JavaScript OLE object.
When clicked it launches an infection chain The attack relies on the user clicking the embedded OLE object to trigger the infection chain.
an infection chain made up of JavaScript, and a Knal shellcode payload Execution of JavaScript from the embedded OLE object to begin the infection process.
that makes use of DNS to load additional shellcode from a remote command and control server Using DNS as a channel to communicate with the command and control (C2) server and load additional shellcode.
this new document variant that makes use of an LNK embedded OLE object The attack starts with a spear-phishing email containing a malicious document with an LNK embedded OLE object.
which extracts a JavaScript bot from a document object, The JavaScript bot is extracted and executed as part of the infection chain.
injects a stealer DLL in memory The stealer DLL is injected directly into memory using PowerShell, allowing the malware to run in the context of legitimate processes.
PowerShell is used to inject the stealer DLL into memory, a key part of the execution chain.
The dropper variant that we encountered makes use of an LNK Kle to execute wscript.exe The dropper leverages a crafted LNK file to initiate the execution of malicious code on the victim's machine, embedded within the Word document.
the beginning of the JavaScript chain The JavaScript chain is used for the next stage of execution
from a word document object The victim likely opens a malicious Word document object, initiating the infection process.

text:
The group still deploys lightweight JavaScript backdoor with communication over HTTPS mimicking Content Delivery Network (CDN) domains
they still leverage JavaScript backdoor via renamedwscript.exe with the actual JavaScript code called "errors.txt," for example
the macro logic copies the original JavaScript execution enginewscript.exe in %LOCALAPPDATA%
checking the system drive size viaGetDrive.TotalSize of more than 2456 bytes to possibly thwart anti-sandbox check
The FIN7 Microsoft document loaders do not rely on any exploits but simply require a social engineering trick to "Enable Content" to activate macros.
The final execution of the backdoor is performed via this following command: %LOCALAPPDATA%mses.exe //b /e:jscript %temp%errors.txt
The actual obfuscated Javascript backdoor is stored in UserForm object, which is also written to a disc as "errors.txt" in%TEMP%
The malware checks for the presence of virtual machine checking whether the malware is running in a virtualized environment
queries active directory
operating system, screen resolution
retrieves a process list.
